notes: The latest Linux Steam client beta allows forcing of Steam Play on native Linux games. See game play here ---> https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dy0LH1YFP0E
title: Dying Light

notes: Works well under PROTON (not native linux version!!!). But you have to cut back on the graphics to have a smooth gaming experience. I disabled PCSS, chromatic abberation and granularity. I set Shadow Map on 'High' (2nd Highest Option).
title: Dying Light

cpu: AMD Ryzen 5 2400G with Radeon Vega Graphics
notes:
Dying Light (Native) uses lspci to detect your Graphics Card (at least when running on mesa). Since on most distributions this binary is located in /usr/sbin/ the game can't find it and reports no video memory and crashes. All i did was creating a symlink "ln -s /usr/sbin/lspci /usr/bin/lspci" and the game would start up fine, even without fixing the library dependencies using ldd on the executable.

Under Proton, the game runs once I disable esync, at a much lower framerate than native (obviously). Before I got the game running natively, this is how I would play. Native is a far superior experience. Disable PCSS, chromatic abberation and granularity for better performance in Proton.
title: Dying Light

notes: I was using an AMD gpu, and did not test any of the NVIDIA specific features. But it worked well, performance seemed like It could be native. Overall worked way better than native which seems to be borked atm.
title: Dying Light

notes:
Game runs near perfectly with proton, however if you experience lag try mucking around with fullscreensettings, disabling nvidia settings, disabling esync, and making sure your monitors are set to the right Hz.  
Can even join windows players for coop.
title: Dying Light

notes: I am posting a new report, because of kernel and driver updates and I have spent significantly more time on it. The game just works, and it works perfectly. The native version is completely borked however. So definitely use proton.
title: Dying Light

notes: Had some issues with OBS causing massive frame drops, but once I tweaked some settings there, it ran buttery smooth. Looks and plays great and online multiplayer works just fine.
title: Dying Light

notes:
This is reviewing the use of Proton, not the native port. Note, the Windows version under Proton runs better and is more stable "for me" on Ubuntu 18.04, Solus Gnome, and Arch Linux.
The Proton version stays well above 100fps for me, on the rare case that a crash occurs, it was due tabbing out of the game to use a browser, but it's rare.
If you're getting stutters on Arch, check "cpupower frequency-info" in the terminal and make sure your CPU isn't turboing down, once I "cpupower frequency-set -d 4GHz" and "cpupower frequency-set -u 5GHz" my games stopped stuttering.
The stutters were occurring because the CPU would randomly drop to 800MHz.
"sudo cpupower frequency-set -g performance" would also work.
title: Dying Light

notes: Proton runs better than the native package, save game also works from steam cloud without any issue.
title: Dying Light

notes:

Works perfectly, just have to tweak graphics to roughly a "medium" to get great fps (I prefer over 60fps).
